title: Community Garden Notice
description: Short community notice for Reading Part 1 practice.
passage: The Riverside Community Garden is open to local residents every Saturday morning from 8 a.m. to 11 a.m. Volunteers help plant vegetables, water flowers and clean shared paths. New members do not need gardening experience because team leaders explain each task at the start of the session. Participants should bring a hat, a water bottle and comfortable shoes. Tools are provided by the garden committee, but children under twelve must come with an adult. This month, the committee is also collecting unused seeds and small plant pots for a school project. Anyone who donates materials will receive two free herb plants to take home.
selection_reason: Part 1 notices train scanning for practical details, conditions and purpose.

title: Email about a CV Workshop
description: Short workplace-study email for Reading Part 1 practice.
passage: Dear students, the Career Center will run a free CV workshop next Tuesday in Room B204. The workshop is designed for students who are applying for internships or part-time jobs. During the first hour, a career adviser will explain how to organize education, skills and work experience clearly. In the second hour, students can ask questions and receive comments on their own CVs. Please bring a printed copy if possible. Places are limited to thirty students, so you should register on the student portal before Friday afternoon. Students who cannot attend will be able to download the slides after the workshop.
selection_reason: Part 1 email texts train detail retrieval and understanding of instructions.

title: Remote Internships
description: Short article for Reading Part 2 practice.
passage: Remote internships have become more common as companies use digital tools to train young workers. Instead of travelling to an office every day, interns join video meetings, complete online tasks and communicate with supervisors through messaging platforms. For students in small towns, this can create opportunities that were previously unavailable. They can gain experience from national or international companies without paying for transport or accommodation. However, remote internships also require strong self-management. Interns may feel isolated if they rarely meet colleagues in person, and it can be harder to ask quick questions. Successful programs usually include clear schedules, regular feedback and online social activities so that interns feel supported rather than forgotten.
selection_reason: Part 2 article supports main idea and inference questions on work and technology.

title: Urban Trees and City Life
description: Short article for Reading Part 2 practice.
passage: Planting trees is one of the simplest ways to improve life in crowded cities. Trees provide shade, reduce street temperatures and absorb some air pollutants. They also help control rainwater because their roots allow water to enter the soil instead of flowing quickly into drains. In many neighborhoods, tree-lined streets encourage people to walk, cycle and spend more time outside. Yet urban tree programs are not always easy to manage. Young trees need regular watering during the first few years, and some species can damage pavements if they are planted in unsuitable places. City planners therefore need to choose local species carefully and involve residents in long-term care.
selection_reason: Environment texts are common in VSTEP reading and support cause-effect comprehension.

title: Digital Payments and Consumer Habits
description: Medium article for Reading Part 3 practice.
passage: Digital payments have changed the way people buy goods and services. In many cities, customers can pay for coffee, transport or groceries with a phone rather than cash. Businesses benefit because transactions are faster and easier to record. Governments also prefer digital payments because they can reduce the size of the informal cash economy. However, the transition is not equally simple for everyone. Older adults may feel uncomfortable using payment apps, while people in rural areas may lack reliable internet access. Privacy is another concern because digital transactions create detailed records of personal behavior. For this reason, financial education and consumer protection rules are important. A cashless society may be efficient, but it should not exclude people who need more support or prefer traditional payment methods.
selection_reason: Part 3 business-technology text trains balanced argument and attitude questions.

title: Sleep and Memory
description: Medium article for Reading Part 3 practice.
passage: Many students sacrifice sleep when examinations approach, believing that extra study hours will improve their performance. Research on memory suggests the opposite may happen. During sleep, the brain strengthens important information and connects it with existing knowledge. This process, known as memory consolidation, is especially important after learning new vocabulary or complex ideas. Lack of sleep reduces attention, slows reaction time and makes it more difficult to solve problems. Short naps can help, but they cannot fully replace regular night-time sleep. Experts recommend a stable sleep schedule, limited caffeine in the evening and a break from bright screens before bed. For learners, sleep should be considered part of the study plan rather than a reward after studying is finished.
selection_reason: Health/learning texts support inference and vocabulary-in-context practice at B2.

title: Language and Cultural Identity
description: Longer analytical text for Reading Part 4 practice.
passage: Language is more than a tool for exchanging information; it is also a carrier of cultural memory and social identity. When people use a local language or dialect, they often express belonging to a particular community and history. Globalization has increased contact between languages, creating new opportunities for communication but also pressure on smaller languages. Some communities fear that younger generations will abandon local speech in favor of global languages associated with education, employment and digital culture. Language preservation, however, is not simply a matter of keeping old words alive. It requires schools, families and media organizations to create meaningful contexts in which the language remains useful. Digital technology can both threaten and support this process: global platforms may spread dominant languages, yet they also allow small communities to publish videos, dictionaries and stories for a wider audience.
selection_reason: Part 4 analytical texts train abstract inference and author-purpose questions for higher-level readers.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare
description: Longer analytical text for Reading Part 4 practice.
passage: Artificial intelligence is increasingly used in healthcare to analyze medical images, predict patient risk and support administrative decisions. Advocates argue that AI can detect patterns that busy doctors might miss and can help hospitals use limited resources more efficiently. For example, an algorithm may identify patients who need urgent follow-up based on thousands of previous cases. Yet healthcare decisions involve more than statistical accuracy. If a system is trained on incomplete or biased data, it may perform less well for certain groups of patients. Transparency is also essential: doctors and patients need to understand why a recommendation has been made, especially when treatment options are serious. The most responsible approach is therefore not to replace medical professionals but to use AI as a carefully monitored tool that supports human judgment.
selection_reason: Part 4 technology-health text supports C1-level evaluation of risk, evidence and accountability.
